have hα : ∀ (a₁ a₂ : α), Pr[=a₁ | $ᵗ α] = Pr[=a₂ | $ᵗ α] := by
      intro a₁ a₂
      have h₁ : Pr[=a₁ | $ᵗ α] = Pr[=a₂ | $ᵗ α] := by
        apply SampleableType.probOutput_selectElem_eq
      exact h₁
    have hβ : ∀ (b₁ b₂ : β), Pr[=b₁ | $ᵗ β] = Pr[=b₂ | $ᵗ β] := by
      intro b₁ b₂
      have h₁ : Pr[=b₁ | $ᵗ β] = Pr[=b₂ | $ᵗ β] := by
        apply SampleableType.probOutput_selectElem_eq
      exact h₁
    have h₁ : Pr[=x | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] = Pr[= (x.1, x.2) | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] := rfl
    have h₂ : Pr[=y | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] = Pr[= (y.1, y.2) | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] := rfl
    rw [h₁, h₂]
    have h₃ : Pr[= (x.1, x.2) | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] = Pr[=x.1 | $ᵗ α] * Pr[=x.2 | $ᵗ β] := by
      simp [probOutput_seq_map_prod_mk_eq_mul]
      <;>
      simp_all [prod.mk.injEq]
      <;>
      aesop
    have h₄ : Pr[= (y.1, y.2) | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] = Pr[=y.1 | $ᵗ α] * Pr[=y.2 | $ᵗ β] := by
      simp [probOutput_seq_map_prod_mk_eq_mul]
      <;>
      simp_all [prod.mk.injEq]
      <;>
      aesop
    rw [h₃, h₄]
    have h₅ : Pr[=x.1 | $ᵗ α] = Pr[=y.1 | $ᵗ α] := hα x.1 y.1
    have h₆ : Pr[=x.2 | $ᵗ β] = Pr[=y.2 | $ᵗ β] := hβ x.2 y.2
    rw [h₅, h₆]
    <;>
    simp_all [mul_assoc]
    <;>
    aesop
  
  probFailure_selectElem : Pr[⊥ | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] = 0 := by
    have h₁ : Pr[⊥ | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] = Pr[⊥ | $ᵗ α] * Pr[⊥ | $ᵗ β] := by

    have h₂ : Pr[⊥ | $ᵗ α] = 0 := SampleableType.probFailure_selectElem
    have h₃ : Pr[⊥ | $ᵗ β] = 0 := SampleableType.probFailure_selectElem
    rw [h₁, h₂, h₃]
    <;> simp
    <;> aesop

intro x y
    simp only [prod_eq]
    -- Since the selection is uniform, the probability of selecting any element is the same.
    -- We use the fact that the cardinality of the product set is the product of the cardinalities.
    have h₁ : Pr[= x.1 | $ᵗ α] = Pr[= y.1 | $ᵗ α] := by
      -- The probability of selecting x.1 is the same as selecting y.1 in α.
      have h₂ : ∀ (x y : α), Pr[= x | $ᵗ α] = Pr[= y | $ᵗ α] := by
        intro x y
        exact SampleableType.probOutput_selectElem_eq x y
      exact h₂ x.1 y.1
    have h₂ : Pr[= x.2 | $ᵗ β] = Pr[= y.2 | $ᵗ β] := by
      -- The probability of selecting x.2 is the same as selecting y.2 in β.
      have h₃ : ∀ (x y : β), Pr[= x | $ᵗ β] = Pr[= y | $ᵗ β] := by
        intro x y
        exact SampleableType.probOutput_selectElem_eq x y
      exact h₃ x.2 y.2
    -- The combined probability is the product of the probabilities in each component.
    calc
      Pr[= x | (·, ·) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = Pr[= (x.1, x.2) | (·, ·) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] := rfl
      _ = Pr[= x.1 | $ᵗ α] * Pr[= x.2 | $ᵗ β] := by
        -- The probability of selecting (x.1, x.2) is the product of the probabilities in each component.
        simp [probOutput_seq_map_prod_mk_eq_mul]
      _ = Pr[= y.1 | $ᵗ α] * Pr[= y.2 | $ᵗ β] := by
        -- Since the probabilities are the same, we can substitute them.
        rw [h₁, h₂]
      _ = Pr[= (y.1, y.2) | (·, ·) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] := by
        -- The probability of selecting (y.1, y.2) is the product of the probabilities in each component.
        simp [probOutput_seq_map_prod_mk_eq_mul]
      _ = Pr[= y | (·, ·) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] := rfl
  
  probFailure_selectElem := by
    -- Since both components have zero probability of failure, their product also has zero probability of failure.
    have h₁ : Pr[⊥ | $ᵗ α] = 0 := SampleableType.probfailure_selectElem
    have h₂ : Pr[⊥ | $ᵗ β] = 0 := SampleableType.probfailure_selectElem

        <;>
        try simp_all [HasEvalSPMF.neverFail_iff]
        <;>
        try aesop

The probability that `(·, ·) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)` outputs `x = (a, b)` is `Pr[= a | $ᵗ α] * Pr[= b | $ᵗ β]`. 

   But for `SampleableType α` and `Fintype α`, we have `Pr[= a | $ᵗ α] = 1 / |α|` (as an ENNReal).

   Therefore, `Pr[= (a, b)] = 1 / (|α| * |β|)`.

   So we need to show that for any `x y : α × β`, `Pr[= x] = Pr[= y]`.

   **But to do this, we need to know that `Pr[= x] = 1 / (|α| * |β|)` for any `x`.**

   And this is not yet in the Lean code, because we have not yet proven that `Pr[= a | $ᵗ α] = 1 / |α|` for `a : α`.

   Actually, for `α : Type` with `[Fintype α]` and `[SampleableType α]`, we do have `Pr[= a | $ᵗ α] = 1 / |α|`, because $ᵗ α is defined to be uniform:
   - in the `SampleableType` instance for `Fintype`, it would use `PMF.uniformOfFintype`.
   - But perhaps we do not have this instance? 

   Looking back, the Lean code only provides `SampleableType` instances for `α` if `Fintype α`, but it seems that such an instance is not imported or included in the given code. 

   So we can't rely on `Pr[= a | $ᵗ α] = 1 / |α|` unless we add that as an instance or prove it.

   Alternatively, perhaps the `SampleableType α` does not guarantee uniform sampling, and it's only the case that all probabilities are equal, but not necessarily `1 / |α|`. No, because if `α` has `n` elements, then

intro x y
    have h₁ : Pr[= x | (fun x1 x2 => (x1, x2)) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ * (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ := by
      have h₂ : Pr[= x | (fun x1 x2 => (x1, x2)) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ * (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ := by
        -- Use the property of product distributions to calculate the probability
        calc
          Pr[= x | (fun x1 x2 => (x1, x2)) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ * (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ := by
            simp [probOutput_seq_map_prod_mk_eq_mul, probOutput_uniformSample]
            <;>
            simp_all [Finset.card_product]
            <;>
            norm_cast
            <;>
            field_simp [mul_comm]
            <;>
            ring_nf
            <;>
            simp_all [Fintype.card_fin]
            <;>
            norm_num
            <;>
            simp_all [mul_comm]
          _ = (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ * (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ := by rfl
      exact h₂
    have h₂ : Pr[= y | (fun x1 x2 => (x1, x2)) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ * (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ := by
      have h₃ : Pr[= y | (fun x1 x2 => (x1, x2)) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ * (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ := by
        -- Use the property of product distributions to calculate the probability
        calc
          Pr[= y | (fun x1 x2 => (x1, x2)) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ * (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ := by
            simp [probOutput_seq_map_prod_mk_eq_mul, probOutput_uniformSample]
            <;>
            simp_all [Finset.card_product]
            <;>
            norm_cast
            <;>

            <;>
            ring_nf
            <;>

            <;>

            <;>

          _ = (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ * (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ := by rfl
      exact h₃
    calc
      (Pr[= x | (fun x1 x2 => (x1, x2)) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)]) = (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ * (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)⁻¹ := by
        exact h₁
      _ = (Pr[= y | (fun x1 x2 => (x1, x2)) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)]) := by
        rw [h₂]

have h₁ : Pr[=x | (·, ·) <$ ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = Pr[=y | (·, ·) <$ ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] := by
      have h₂ : ∀ (p q : α × β), Pr[=p | (·, ·) <$ ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = Pr[=q | (·, ·) <$ ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] := by
        intro p q
        -- Use the fact that the probabilities are uniformly distributed and the product structure
        -- to show that the probabilities of any two outcomes are equal.
        -- This relies on the uniformity of the sample space and the independence of the two samples.
        have h₃ : Pr[=p | (·, ·) <$ ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = (1 / (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)) * (1 / (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)) := by
          simp [probOutput_seqMap_prod_mk, probOutput_uniformSample]

          norm_cast <;>
          field_simp [Fintype.card_pos_iff] <;>
          ring_nf <;>

          <;>

          (try linarith)
          <;>
          (try simp_all [Fintype.card_pos_iff])
          <;>
          (try norm_num)
          <;>
          (try linarith)
        have h₄ : Pr[=q | (·, ·) <$ ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = (1 / (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)) * (1 / (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)) := by
          simp [probOutput_seqMap_prod_mk, probOutput_uniformSample]
          <;>

          norm_cast <;>
          field_simp [Fintype.card_pos_iff] <;>
          ring_nf <;>

          <;>

          <;>
          (try norm_num)
          <;>
          (try linarith)
        calc
          Pr[=p | (·, ·) <$ ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = (1 / (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)) * (1 / (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)) := h₃
          _ = (1 / (Fintype.card α : ℝ≥0∞)) * (1 / (Fintype.card β : ℝ≥0∞)) := by rfl
          _ = Pr[=q | (·, ·) <$ ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] := by rw [h₄]
      -- Apply the general statement to the specific cases of x and y.
      have h₅ := h₂ x y
      exact h₅
    exact h₁

    have h₁ : Pr[⊥ | (·, ·) <$> ($ᵗ α) *< ($ᵗ β)] = 0 := by

        <;>

to take it as `(M1.bind (λ r1 => if r1 = a then (M2.bind (λ r2 => if r2 = b then 1 else 0)).some else 0)).some`.

But by definition, `(M2.bind (λ r2 => if r2 = b then 1 else 0)).some = Pr[=b | M2]`, which is independent of `r1`.

Thus:

`= (M1.bind (λ r1 => if r1 = a then c else 0)).some`, where `c = Pr[=b | M2]`.

Similarly, this is `c * Pr[=a | M1]`.

Similarly, the other side is `c' * Pr[=a' | M1]`, where `c' = Pr[=b' | M2]`.

But since `Pr[=a | M1] = Pr[=a' | M1]` and `Pr[=b | M2] = Pr[=b' | M2]`, we have `c = c'`, and thus equality.

### Step 5: Formalizing the Proof

But perhaps in Lean, it would be easier to use the fact that `Pr[=x | M]` can be computed as a product, and then to use `probOutput_selectElem_eq`.

Alternatively, perhaps it already exists in Lean's library that for a product, the probability is the product of probabilities. Let me try to find it.

Actually, in Lean's `SPMF`, we have:
`probOutput_map_eq_sum` but it is only for `Fintype`.

But perhaps `probOutput_map_eq_sum` could be generalized to use `tsum`.

Alternatively, perhaps we can use that for any two `x y`, the probabilities are the same, because, for `(a,b)`, the probabilities `P[=a]` and `P[=b]` are the same for any pairs, and the product is symmetric.

But we must be careful: If Lean uses `OptionT`, then perhaps it is better to use `OptionT.bind` and products. 

Alternatively, perhaps it is better to directly use `probOutput_seq_map_prod_mk_eq_mul`.

Oh! Looking in Lean's library, there is actually a lemma `probOutput_seq_map_prod_mk_eq_mul`:

```
theorem probOutput_seq_map_prod_mk_eq_mul {α β : Type} {X : SPMF α} {Y : SPMF β} {a : α} {b : β} :
    Pr[(a, b) | X * Y] = Pr[a | X] * Pr[b | Y] := by
  ...
```

But in Lean, `X * Y` is `bind X (λ x => Y.map (λ y => (x, y)))`.

Thus, `(·,·) <;> X <;> Y = X * Y`.

Thus, we have:
`Pr[= (a,b) | X * Y] = Pr[=a | X] * Pr[=b | Y]`.

Similarly for `(a', b')` we get `Pr[=a' | X] * Pr[=b' | Y]`.

But because of `probOutput_selectElem_eq`, `Pr[=a | X] = Pr[=a' | X]` and similarly for `b`, we get that the products are equal.

But Lean's instance requires that `X` and `Y` are `sample α` and `sample β`, which are `SPMF`s.

  [Unique

-- We need to show that the probability of selecting x is the same as selecting y.
    have h₁ : Pr[=x | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] = Pr[=y | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] := by
      -- Use the fact that the probabilities of selecting the components are the same.
      have h₂ : Pr[=x | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] = Pr[=y | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] := by
        -- Use the fact that the probabilities of selecting the components are the same.
        calc
          Pr[=x | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] = (Pr[=x.1 | $ᵗ α] * Pr[=x.2 | $ᵗ β]) := by
            -- Use the fact that the probabilities of selecting the components are the same.
            simp [probOutput_map_prod, probOutput_and, mul_comm]
            <;>
            (try aesop)
            <;>
            (try simp_all [sampleableType])
            <;>
            (try ring_nf)
            <;>
            (try aesop)
          _ = (Pr[=y.1 | $ᵗ α] * Pr[=y.2 | $ᵗ β]) := by
            -- Use the fact that the probabilities of selecting the components are the same.
            have h₃ : Pr[=x.1 | $ᵗ α] = Pr[=y.1 | $ᵗ α] := by
              -- Use the fact that the probabilities of selecting the components are the same.
              have h₄ : ∀ (a b : α), Pr[=a | $ᵗ α] = Pr[=b | $ᵗ α] := by
                intro a b
                have h₅ := SampleableType.probOutput_selectElem_eq a b
                simpa using h₅
              exact h₄ x.1 y.1
            have h₄ : Pr[=x.2 | $ᵗ β] = Pr[=y.2 | $ᵗ β] := by
              -- Use the fact that the probabilities of selecting the components are the same.
              have h₅ : ∀ (a b : β), Pr[=a | $ᵗ β] = Pr[=b | $ᵗ β] := by
                intro a b
                have h₆ := SampleableType.probOutput_selectElem_eq a b
                simpa using h₆
              exact h₅ x.2 y.2
            calc
              (Pr[=x.1 | $ᵗ α] * Pr[=x.2 | $ᵗ β]) = (Pr[=y.1 | $ᵗ α] * Pr[=x.2 | $ᵗ β]) := by rw [h₃]
              _ = (Pr[=y.1 | $ᵗ α] * Pr[=y.2 | $ᵗ β]) := by rw [h₄]
          _ = Pr[=y | (·, ·) <$> $ᵗ α <*> $ᵗ β] := by
            -- Use the fact that the probabilities of selecting the components are the same.
            simp [probOutput_map_prod, probOutput_and, mul_comm]
            <;>
            (try aesop)
            <;>
            (try simp_all [sampleableType])
            <;>
            (try ring_nf)
            <;>
            (try aesop)
      exact h₂
    exact h₁
  
  probFailure_selectElem : Pr[⊥ | (·, ·) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = 0 := by
    have h₁ : Pr[⊥ | (·, ·) <$> ($ᵗ α) <*> ($ᵗ β)] = 0 := by
      simp [probFailure_impure]
      <;>
      (try simp_all [sampleableType])
      <;>
      (try aesop)
      <;>
      (try norm_num)
      <;>
      (try simp_all [sampleableType])
      <;>
      (try aesop)
    exact h₁
